label: vulnerable

source.description: User-controlled string from query parameter 'filename' received in the route layer. No type, format, or character check is applied.

propagation.description: filename is forwarded unchanged to FileService.get_file_content() — no validation at the route-to-service boundary.
FileService.get_file_content() passes filename directly to read_file() without any sanitization — no validation at the service-to-utils boundary.
os.path.join(BASE_DIR, filename) appends the tainted string. A value containing '../' sequences resolves outside BASE_DIR.

sink.description: open() reads the file at the unvalidated path. An attacker can supply '../../etc/passwd' to read arbitrary files accessible to the process.

missing_controls: os.path.realpath() to canonicalize symlinks and '..' components
prefix restriction: assert resolved path starts with os.path.realpath(BASE_DIR)
validation at service or utils boundary before os.path.join
expected_findings.risk_reasoning: The filename value propagates through three layers (route → service → utils) without any validation. It enters os.path.join and then open() in the utils layer. '../' traversal sequences are not stripped at any layer, enabling arbitrary file read.
expected_findings.remediation_hint: In read_file(), resolve the joined path with os.path.realpath() and assert it starts with os.path.realpath(BASE_DIR) + os.sep before calling open(). Alternatively, add the check at the service boundary before delegating to utils.

recall_anchor.description: A correct system must identify the sink in utils/file_utils.py. Source identification in routes/file_routes.py is additional credit.

notes: This case tests cross-file inter-procedural taint tracing: route layer (source) → service layer (passthrough) → utils layer (sink). Each layer boundary is an opportunity for a system to lose track of the taint.
